name: Hydrogen
description: Hydrogen is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe. It makes up about 75% of all normal matter by mass. On Earth, it exists mostly in compounds like water (H₂O).
funFact: The Sun burns 600 million tonnes of hydrogen every second through nuclear fusion!

name: Helium
description: Helium is a colourless, odourless, tasteless, non-toxic, inert monatomic gas. It is the first noble gas and the second lightest element. Helium has the lowest boiling point of all elements.
funFact: Helium was discovered in the Sun before it was found on Earth — its name comes from Helios, the Greek god of the Sun.

name: Lithium
description: Lithium is a soft, silvery-white alkali metal. It is the lightest metal and the lightest solid element. Like all alkali metals, lithium is highly reactive and flammable.
funFact: Lithium reacts so vigorously with water that it must be stored in mineral oil to prevent it from catching fire!

name: Beryllium
description: Beryllium is a steel-grey, hard, lightweight alkaline earth metal. It is relatively rare in the universe and in the Earth's crust. Beryllium is non-magnetic and has excellent thermal conductivity.
funFact: Beryllium is used in the James Webb Space Telescope's mirrors because it's stiff, light, and doesn't warp at extreme cold temperatures.

name: Boron
discoveredBy: Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac, Louis Jacques Thénard
description: Boron is a metalloid that is hard and brittle in its pure form. It occurs naturally as borates in the Earth's crust. Boron is an essential micronutrient for plants and is found in soil worldwide.
funFact: Boron carbide is one of the hardest known materials — harder than steel — and is used in tank armour and bulletproof vests.

name: Carbon
description: Carbon is the basis of all known life on Earth. It can form more compounds than any other element because of its unique ability to form stable chains and rings. Its allotropes include graphite, diamond, and graphene.
funFact: A single gram of carbon contains about 50 sextillion atoms — that's 50,000,000,000,000,000,000,000!

name: Nitrogen
description: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere by volume. It is a colourless, odourless, largely inert diatomic gas at standard conditions. Nitrogen is essential for all living organisms as a component of amino acids and DNA.
funFact: Lightning strikes convert nitrogen gas into a form plants can absorb — nature's way of making fertilizer!

name: Oxygen
description: Oxygen is the third most abundant element in the universe and the most abundant element in the Earth's crust. It makes up about 21% of Earth's atmosphere. It is essential for respiration in most living organisms.
funFact: Liquid oxygen is pale blue and magnetic — it will stick to a magnet!

name: Fluorine
description: Fluorine is the most electronegative and one of the most reactive elements. It is a pale yellow, poisonous, corrosive gas at room temperature. Fluorine forms compounds with almost every element, including noble gases.
funFact: Fluorine is so reactive that it can set fire to glass, concrete, and even water under the right conditions!

name: Neon
description: Neon is a noble gas that glows a distinctive reddish-orange when used in gas-discharge lamps. It is produced by fractional distillation of liquid air. Neon is the fifth most abundant element in the universe.
funFact: Despite being famous for 'neon signs', most coloured signs actually use other gases — only the orange-red colour is true neon!

name: Sodium
description: Sodium is a soft, silvery-white, highly reactive alkali metal. It is the sixth most abundant element in the Earth's crust. Sodium is essential in the human body for nerve function and maintaining fluid balance.
funFact: Sodium explodes violently when dropped in water — the reaction produces hydrogen gas that ignites instantly!

name: Magnesium
description: Magnesium is a shiny grey solid which shares many physical and chemical properties with the other five alkaline earth metals. It is the eighth most abundant element in the Earth's crust and essential for chlorophyll in plants.
funFact: Magnesium burns with such a bright white flame that it was used in early photography as 'flash powder'!

name: Aluminium
description: Aluminium is a silvery-white, lightweight metal. It is the most abundant metal and the third most abundant element in the Earth's crust. Aluminium is highly resistant to corrosion due to the oxide layer that forms on its surface.
funFact: Aluminium was once more valuable than gold — Napoleon III served his most honoured guests on aluminium plates while others used gold ones!

name: Silicon
description: Silicon is a hard, brittle crystalline solid metalloid. It is the second most abundant element in the Earth's crust (after oxygen). Silicon is the basis of the modern electronics industry — it is the key component of semiconductors.
funFact: The Silicon Valley is named after silicon because nearly all computer chips are made from it!

name: Phosphorus
description: Phosphorus is a highly reactive nonmetal. White phosphorus glows in the dark (hence the name, from Greek for 'light-bearer'). Red phosphorus is less reactive and is used in safety matches. It is essential for DNA, RNA, and ATP in living organisms.
funFact: Phosphorus was discovered by an alchemist boiling down 50 buckets of urine — he was trying to make gold!

name: Sulfur
description: Sulfur is a bright yellow, odourless, brittle solid at room temperature. It is an abundant, multivalent nonmetal and has been known since ancient times. Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) has the characteristic smell of rotten eggs.
funFact: Sulfur smells terrible, but without it, your hair would have no strength — sulfur bonds hold your hair's keratin together!

name: Chlorine
description: Chlorine is a yellow-green gas that is extremely reactive. It is the second lightest halogen. Chlorine is widely used for purifying water and as a disinfectant. Sodium chloride (table salt) is its most common compound.
funFact: Chlorine was used as a chemical weapon in World War I, but today it saves millions of lives each year by purifying drinking water.

name: Argon
description: Argon is the third most abundant gas in the Earth's atmosphere. It is inert, colourless, and odourless. Because it is completely chemically inert, it is used to protect reactive materials from oxygen and moisture.
funFact: About 1% of every breath you take is argon — and you breathe it out completely unchanged because it doesn't react with anything in your body.

name: Potassium
description: Potassium is a soft, silvery-white metal that belongs to the alkali metal group. Its symbol K comes from the Latin word 'kalium'. It is essential for human health — our cells use potassium ions to transmit electrical signals in nerves and muscles.
funFact: A banana contains about 422 mg of potassium. Your body needs potassium so badly that without it, your heart would stop beating!

name: Calcium
description: Calcium is the fifth most abundant element in the Earth's crust. It is an essential component of bones, teeth, and shells. It is a reactive metal that, when freshly cut, appears silver-white but quickly oxidises in air.
funFact: Your body contains about 1 kg of calcium — almost all of it stored in your bones and teeth!

name: Scandium
description: Scandium is a silvery-white metallic d-block element. It is relatively rare and not widely used in bulk, but it is used in speciality alloys. It was predicted by Mendeleev before its discovery.
funFact: Scandium was predicted by Mendeleev as 'ekaboron' before its discovery — proof that the periodic table works like a crystal ball!

name: Titanium
description: Titanium is a lustrous transition metal with a silver colour and low density. It is extremely strong for its weight and highly corrosion-resistant. It is biocompatible, meaning the human body tolerates it well.
funFact: Titanium is so biocompatible that bone cells will actually grow directly onto implanted titanium — your skeleton bonds with it!

name: Vanadium
description: Vanadium is a hard, silvery-grey, ductile transition metal. It occurs in about 65 different minerals. Adding small amounts to steel creates an extremely strong alloy used in tools and structural steel.
funFact: Vanadium was named after Vanadis (Freyja), the Norse goddess of beauty — its compounds form strikingly colourful solutions!

name: Chromium
description: Chromium is a steely-grey lustrous metal. It is the main component in stainless steel, giving it corrosion resistance. Chromium compounds are brilliantly coloured and used as pigments.
funFact: Rubies get their red colour from trace amounts of chromium! Emeralds also contain chromium but appear green instead.

name: Manganese
description: Manganese is a hard, brittle, silvery metal. It is an essential trace element in the diet of animals. Manganese is widely used in steel production to improve hardness and toughness.
funFact: Manganese has the most oxidation states of any element — it can exist in 9 different states from -3 to +7!

name: Iron
description: Iron is the most common element by mass in the Earth and fourth most common in the Earth's crust. It is the main component of steel and has been used by humans for thousands of years. Iron is vital in biology as part of haemoglobin in red blood cells.
funFact: The Earth's solid inner core is a ball of iron and nickel the size of the Moon, spinning inside a liquid iron outer core!

name: Cobalt
description: Cobalt is a hard, lustrous, silver-grey metal. It is ferromagnetic and is used to make permanent magnets. Cobalt compounds have been used since ancient times as blue pigments in glass and ceramics.
funFact: Vitamin B12 — essential for your nervous system — contains cobalt at its core. It's the only vitamin with a metal in it!

name: Nickel
description: Nickel is a silvery-white lustrous metal with a slight golden tinge. It is corrosion-resistant, takes a high polish, and is used in many alloys. Nickel is an important component of stainless steel
funFact: The US five-cent coin is called a 'nickel' but only contains 25% nickel — the rest is copper!

name: Copper
description: Copper is a soft, malleable, and ductile metal with very high thermal and electrical conductivity. It has a distinctive reddish-orange colour. Copper has been used by humans for over 10,000 years and was one of the first metals ever smelted.
funFact: The Statue of Liberty is made of copper and has turned green because of oxidation — originally it was a shiny reddish-brown!

name: Zinc
description: Zinc is a slightly brittle metal at room temperature and has a silvery-greyish appearance. It is used to galvanise other metals such as iron to prevent rusting. Zinc is an essential mineral for human health, involved in immune function and wound healing.
funFact: Galvanized steel — coated with zinc — is what most metal poles, fences, and buckets are made of. The zinc sacrifices itself to protect the iron!

name: Gallium
description: Gallium is a soft, silvery metal that melts just above room temperature (29.76°C). It is liquid for much of the day in warm climates. Gallium is a key material in semiconductors and LED technology.
funFact: Gallium melts right in your hand at body temperature — it literally turns into a liquid when you hold it!

name: Germanium
description: Germanium is a lustrous, hard-brittle, grey-white metalloid in the carbon group. Mendeleev predicted its existence years before its discovery, calling it 'ekasilicon'. It was the first material used in transistors.
funFact: Germanium was predicted by Mendeleev 15 years before it was discovered — and its properties matched his predictions almost perfectly!

name: Arsenic
description: Arsenic is a metalloid that can exist as several allotropes, the most common being grey arsenic. It is notorious as a poison, but small amounts are essential trace elements in some animals. Arsenic compounds are used in pesticides and semiconductors.
funFact: Victorian wallpaper was often coloured with arsenic-based green pigment — breathing the fumes in damp houses was thought to have poisoned Napoleon Bonaparte!

name: Selenium
description: Selenium is a nonmetal with properties that are intermediate between those of its periodic table neighbours tellurium and sulfur. It is an essential trace element in the diet. Its electrical conductivity increases with light exposure, making it useful in photocopiers.
funFact: Selenium was named after the Moon (Selene in Greek) because it was always found alongside tellurium, which was named after the Earth.

name: Bromine
description: Bromine is the only nonmetal that is a liquid at room temperature (besides mercury, a metal). It is a dark reddish-brown liquid with suffocating fumes. Bromine is quite reactive and has many important industrial applications.
funFact: Bromine is one of only two elements that are liquid at room temperature — the other is mercury!

name: Krypton
description: Krypton is a colourless, odourless noble gas that occurs in trace amounts in the atmosphere. Its name comes from the Greek 'kryptos' meaning hidden. Krypton is used in some types of photographic flashes and fluorescent lights.
funFact: Superman's weakness, Kryptonite, is named after real krypton — though the real element certainly won't hurt you!

name: Rubidium
description: Rubidium is a very soft, silvery-white alkali metal. It is highly reactive and catches fire spontaneously in air. Its name comes from the Latin 'rubidus' (dark red) because of the red spectral lines used to discover it.
funFact: Rubidium atomic clocks are so accurate that they lose about 1 second every 300 years — they're used to calibrate GPS satellites!

name: Strontium
description: Strontium is a soft silver-white yellowish metallic element that is highly chemically reactive. It burns bright crimson red, which is why it is used in fireworks and emergency flares.
funFact: Strontium gives fireworks their brilliant crimson-red colour — look for it in every red firework you see!

name: Yttrium
description: Yttrium is a silvery-metallic transition metal. It is never found in nature as a free element. Yttrium is commonly used in making phosphors — the compounds that create colours in television screens and LEDs.
funFact: Four elements were named after the Swedish village of Ytterby where they were discovered: Yttrium, Ytterbium, Erbium, and Terbium!

name: Zirconium
description: Zirconium is a lustrous, greyish-white, soft, ductile and malleable metal. It is exceptionally resistant to corrosion. Zirconium dioxide (cubic zirconia) is used as a diamond substitute in jewellery.
funFact: Cubic zirconia, the fake diamond used in jewellery, is made from zirconium dioxide — it's visually almost indistinguishable from real diamond!

name: Niobium
description: Niobium is a light grey, crystalline, and ductile transition metal. It becomes a superconductor at low temperatures. Adding niobium to steel produces high-strength alloys used in pipelines and automotive applications.
funFact: Niobium turns beautiful colours — gold, blue, green, purple — when anodized, which is why it's used in jewellery and art!

name: Molybdenum
description: Molybdenum is a silvery metal with a high melting point. It does not occur naturally as a free metal on Earth. It is an essential trace element for nearly all life forms — it is required by nitrogen-fixing bacteria that put nitrogen into the soil.
funFact: Molybdenum has the fifth highest melting point of all elements — it was once confused with graphite because they look similar!

name: Technetium
description: Technetium was the first element to be produced artificially. All of its isotopes are radioactive. Despite this, it has important medical applications. The name comes from the Greek 'technetos' meaning artificial.
funFact: Technetium is the lightest element with no stable isotopes — yet it saves thousands of lives daily as a medical imaging agent!

name: Ruthenium
description: Ruthenium is a rare transition metal belonging to the platinum group. It is hard and brittle and has four crystal modifications. It is used to harden platinum and palladium and as an efficient catalyst.
funFact: A tiny amount of ruthenium added to titanium makes it 100 times more corrosion-resistant — a little goes a very long way!

name: Rhodium
description: Rhodium is a hard, silvery, durable metal that has a high reflectance. It is in the platinum group and is one of the rarest and most valuable precious metals on Earth.
funFact: Rhodium is the most expensive metal in the world — far more costly than gold or platinum — and your car's catalytic converter uses it!

name: Palladium
description: Palladium is a rare and lustrous silvery-white metal discovered in 1803. It is a member of the platinum group. Notably, it can absorb up to 900 times its own volume of hydrogen at room temperature.
funFact: Palladium can absorb 900 times its own volume in hydrogen — scientists once hoped this property could enable cold fusion!

name: Silver
description: Silver is a soft, white lustrous metal that has the highest electrical conductivity, the highest thermal conductivity, and the highest reflectivity of any metal. It has been valued as a precious metal for millennia.
funFact: Silver has the highest electrical conductivity of any element — but copper is used in wiring because silver is too expensive!

name: Cadmium
description: Cadmium is a soft, bluish-white metal that is chemically similar to zinc. It is toxic and its use is declining due to environmental concerns. However, it remains important in nickel-cadmium rechargeable batteries.
funFact: Cadmium yellow was once a favourite pigment of artists — Van Gogh and Monet used it — but we now know it's highly toxic!

name: Indium
description: Indium is a post-transition metal that makes up 0.21 parts per million of the Earth's crust. It is a very soft, silvery-white metal. Indium tin oxide (ITO) is transparent and conductive, making it essential for touchscreens.
funFact: Every touchscreen you've ever used likely contains indium — it's the element that makes touch displays possible!

name: Tin
description: Tin is a silvery metal that characteristically has a faint yellow hue. It has the most stable isotopes of any element. Bronze — an alloy of tin and copper — was so important it gave its name to the Bronze Age.
funFact: When you bend a tin bar, it makes a creaking sound called 'tin cry' — caused by tin crystals scrunching against each other!

name: Antimony
description: Antimony is a lustrous grey metalloid. It is found in over 100 mineral species. Antimony and its compounds are used in flame-proofing compounds, batteries, and semiconductors. Its symbol Sb comes from the Latin 'stibium'.
funFact: Ancient Egyptians used antimony sulfide (kohl) as eye makeup — Cleopatra may have lined her eyes with antimony!

name: Tellurium
description: Tellurium is a brittle, mildly toxic, rare, silver-white metalloid. It is far more common in the Universe than on Earth. It is used in solar cells and as a colouring agent in glass and ceramics.
funFact: Even tiny amounts of tellurium ingested will make your breath and body smell of garlic for weeks — it's nearly impossible to get rid of!

name: Iodine
description: Iodine is a non-metallic, lustrous, purple-black solid at room temperature. It sublimes to a beautiful violet gas when heated. It is an essential micronutrient — without enough iodine, the thyroid gland swells into a goitre.
funFact: Iodine sublimates directly from solid to purple-violet gas without becoming liquid — it's used as a test for starch, turning deep blue-black on contact!

name: Xenon
description: Xenon is a colourless, dense, odourless noble gas found in Earth's atmosphere in trace amounts. Although it was thought to be completely inert, xenon compounds do exist. It is used in bright flashing lights such as car headlights.
funFact: Xenon can be used as an anaesthetic — inhaling it puts you to sleep without any side effects, unlike most anaesthetics!

name: Caesium
description: Caesium is a soft, silvery-gold alkali metal with a melting point of 28.5°C — it becomes liquid on a warm day. It is highly reactive. It has the lowest electronegativity of all non-radioactive elements.
funFact: The international definition of a 'second' is based on caesium! One second = 9,192,631,770 oscillations of caesium-133 atoms.

name: Barium
description: Barium is a soft, silvery alkaline earth metal. Because of its high chemical reactivity, barium is never found as a free element in nature. It is used as a 'getter' in vacuum tubes to remove trace gases.
funFact: When you have an X-ray of your digestive system, you swallow a barium sulfate 'milkshake' — barium absorbs X-rays and makes your gut visible!

name: Lanthanum
description: Lanthanum is a soft, ductile, silvery-white metal that tarnishes rapidly when exposed to air. It is the first element in the lanthanide series. It is used in camera and telescope lenses to produce a special glass that reduces chromatic aberration.
funFact: High-end camera lenses often contain lanthanum oxide to improve optical clarity — your photos may have been taken through lanthanum!

name: Cerium
description: Cerium is a soft, ductile and silvery-white metal that tarnishes when exposed to air. It is the most abundant of the rare earth elements. Cerium compounds are used as catalysts and as polishing agents for glass.
funFact: The sparks you see from a lighter come from a 'misch metal' alloy containing mostly cerium — cerium sparks when struck!

name: Praseodymium
description: Praseodymium is a soft, silvery, malleable and ductile metal. It is a member of the lanthanide series. Its name means 'green twin' in Greek, referring to the green colour of its salts. It is used in strong permanent magnets.
funFact: Praseodymium is used in goggles worn by glassblowers — its compounds absorb the harmful yellow flare produced by sodium in hot glass!

name: Neodymium
description: Neodymium is a bright silvery metallic element. Neodymium magnets (NdFeB) are the strongest type of permanent magnets made. They are found in hard disk drives, electric motors, and MRI machines.
funFact: Neodymium magnets are so strong that a fridge-sized block would be impossible to separate by hand — they can crush fingers if they snap together!

name: Promethium
discoveredBy: Jacob A. Marinsky, Lawrence E. Glendenin
description: Promethium is an extremely rare radioactive rare-earth element. All isotopes are radioactive. It does not occur naturally on Earth in significant amounts and must be made artificially. It was the last naturally occurring element to be discovered.
funFact: Promethium is so rare that only about 500 grams exist on Earth at any one time — all of it artificially made in nuclear reactors!

name: Samarium
description: Samarium is a moderately hard silvery metal. It is used in making powerful samarium-cobalt magnets (the second strongest type of permanent magnet). It also has applications in cancer treatment.
funFact: Samarium-cobalt magnets were used in the weapons guidance systems of early 'smart bombs' — tiny but incredibly powerful magnets!

name: Europium
description: Europium is a moderately hard, silvery metal. It is the most reactive of the rare earth metals. Europium is used as a red and blue phosphor in colour TV tubes and as a security feature in euro banknotes.
funFact: Euro banknotes contain europium compounds that glow under UV light — it's Europe's built-in anti-counterfeiting secret!

name: Gadolinium
description: Gadolinium is a silvery-white, malleable and ductile rare-earth metal. It has the highest neutron absorption capability of all known elements. Gadolinium-based compounds are used as MRI contrast agents.
funFact: When you get an MRI scan with contrast dye, it almost certainly contains gadolinium — it's injected into your veins to enhance the images!

name: Terbium
description: Terbium is a silvery-white, rare earth metal that is malleable, ductile, and soft enough to be cut with a knife. It is used in green phosphors and in making magnetostrictive alloys that change shape in magnetic fields.
funFact: Terbium makes sonar equipment more sensitive — alloys containing terbium expand and contract with magnetic fields, creating sound waves!

name: Dysprosium
description: Dysprosium is a soft, silvery metal. Its name comes from the Greek 'dysprositos' meaning hard to get. It has a high magnetic susceptibility and is added to neodymium magnets to keep them strong at higher temperatures.
funFact: Electric cars and wind turbines need dysprosium in their magnets to keep working in heat — it's a hidden hero of the green energy revolution!

name: Holmium
description: Holmium is a part of the lanthanide series. It is a rare-earth element and has the highest magnetic moment of any naturally occurring element. It is named after Holmia, the Latinized name for Stockholm.
funFact: Holmium has the strongest magnetic moment of any element — which makes it useful for creating the most powerful magnetic fields in labs!

name: Erbium
description: Erbium is a silvery-white solid metal when artificially isolated. It is used mostly in phosphors, glass colouring, and in fibre optic telecommunications. Its rose-pink spectral emission is characteristic.
funFact: The internet runs on erbium! Erbium-doped fibre amplifiers boost light signals in undersea internet cables spanning the entire ocean.

name: Thulium
description: Thulium is the least abundant of the rare earth elements that naturally occur. It is an easily workable metal with a bright silvery-gray lustre. Thulium is used in portable X-ray devices and surgical lasers.
funFact: Thulium is so rare that almost no one has ever seen it — yet it's used in portable X-ray machines that don't need electricity!

name: Ytterbium
description: Ytterbium is a soft, malleable and ductile chemical element that displays a bright silvery lustre when pure. It is a rare earth element. Ytterbium has potential applications in atomic clocks more accurate than caesium clocks.
funFact: Ytterbium atoms make the most accurate clocks ever made — losing or gaining less than one second in 15 billion years!

name: Lutetium
description: Lutetium is a silvery-white metal. It is the last element in the lanthanide series. It is one of the rarest and most expensive of the rare earth metals. It has potential use in PET scan detectors.
funFact: Lutetium is named after Lutetia, the Roman name for Paris — and it's one of the most expensive metals, costing more than gold per gram!

name: Hafnium
description: Hafnium is a lustrous, silvery grey, tetravalent transition metal. It closely resembles zirconium and is very resistant to corrosion. Hafnium is a member of the refractory metals, which are exceptionally resistant to heat and wear.
funFact: Hafnium was one of the last stable elements to be discovered — found in 1923, it had been hiding inside zirconium samples for decades!

name: Tantalum
description: Tantalum is a rare, hard, blue-grey, lustrous transition metal. It is highly corrosion-resistant. Tantalum is chemically inert, making it ideal for surgical and dental implants. Columbite-tantalite ('coltan') ore is a key source.
funFact: Your smartphone likely contains tantalum in its capacitors — 'conflict minerals' from tantalum mining have fuelled wars in the Congo.

name: Tungsten
description: Tungsten has the highest melting point of all elements. It is a hard, steel-grey metal with the highest melting point of any pure metal (3,422°C or 6,192°F). Its symbol W comes from the German 'Wolfram'.
funFact: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any element — it could sit in the flames of a furnace while still remaining solid!

name: Rhenium
description: Rhenium is a silvery-white, heavy, third-row transition metal. It was one of the last stable elements to be discovered. It has one of the highest melting points of all elements and the highest boiling point.
funFact: Rhenium has the highest boiling point of any element — a staggering 5,596°C — and it makes jet engine turbine blades possible!

name: Osmium
description: Osmium is the densest naturally occurring element. It is a hard, brittle, bluish-white transition metal. It has a pungent smell from osmium tetroxide, which is highly toxic. It belongs to the platinum group.
funFact: Osmium is the densest element in the universe — a cube of it the size of a sugar cube would weigh as much as a full water bottle!

name: Iridium
description: Iridium is a very hard, brittle, silvery-white transition metal of the platinum group. It has the second highest density and the highest corrosion-resistance of all metals. A thin layer of iridium marks the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary.
funFact: The asteroid that killed the dinosaurs left a thin layer of iridium in rock worldwide — it's how scientists pinpointed when the impact occurred!

name: Platinum
description: Platinum is a dense, malleable, ductile, highly unreactive, precious, silverish-white transition metal. It is one of the rarer elements in the Earth's crust and occurs in some nickel and copper ores. It is used extensively in jewellery and catalytic converters.
funFact: The original international standard for the kilogram was a platinum-iridium cylinder stored in Paris — the most important lump of metal ever made!

name: Gold
description: Gold is a bright, slightly reddish yellow, dense, soft, malleable, and ductile metal. It is one of the least reactive chemical elements. Gold has been a symbol of wealth and power since ancient civilisations. It occurs naturally in pure form.
funFact: All the gold ever mined in human history would fit into a cube about 21 metres on each side — less than 3 Olympic swimming pools!

name: Mercury
description: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at standard room temperature and pressure. It is a heavy, silvery d-block element. Mercury is toxic to living beings. The Mad Hatter in Alice in Wonderland was inspired by hat makers who developed mercury poisoning.
funFact: The term 'Mad Hatter' comes from 19th-century hatmakers who used mercury in felt-making and suffered neurological damage from the fumes!

name: Thallium
description: Thallium is a soft grey post-transition metal. It is highly toxic and was once used as a rat poison. It is found in trace amounts in the Earth's crust and is produced as a by-product of heavy metal sulfide ore smelting.
funFact: Thallium was called 'the poisoner's poison' because it's tasteless, colourless, and causes hair loss — making it the perfect murder weapon in detective novels!

name: Lead
description: Lead is a heavy metal that is denser than most common materials. It is soft, malleable, and has a relatively low melting point. Lead was used extensively historically in plumbing, paints, and fuels, but is now known to be highly toxic to humans.
funFact: Some historians believe lead poisoning contributed to the fall of the Roman Empire — Romans used lead pots and pipes and added lead compounds to sweeten wine!

name: Bismuth
description: Bismuth is a brittle metal with a silvery white colour when freshly produced, but surface oxidation gives it a pink tinge. It is often used as a replacement for lead due to its low toxicity. Pepto-Bismol, the stomach medicine, contains bismuth.
funFact: Bismuth forms stunning hopper crystals with iridescent rainbow surfaces — they look like tiny rainbow staircases and you can grow them at home!

name: Polonium
description: Polonium is a radioactive element that was discovered by Marie Curie, who named it after her homeland Poland. It is extremely rare in nature. Polonium-210 is one of the most toxic substances known — it was used to poison Alexander Litvinenko.
funFact: Marie Curie named polonium after Poland — she was the first to discover it and it was the first element named after a country!

name: Astatine
discoveredBy: Dale R. Corson, Kenneth Ross MacKenzie, Emilio Segrè
description: Astatine is a radioactive chemical element that is the rarest naturally occurring element in the Earth's crust. It is a halogen and the heaviest naturally occurring element. At any given time, less than 1 gram exists naturally on Earth.
funFact: Astatine is so rare that less than 1 gram of it exists naturally on Earth at any moment — it constantly decays back into other elements!

name: Radon
description: Radon is a radioactive, colourless, odourless, tasteless noble gas. It occurs naturally from the decay of radium. It is the heaviest naturally occurring noble gas. Radon seeps into buildings through soil and rock and is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking.
funFact: Radon is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking — it seeps invisibly from rocks and soil into homes, and you can't smell or see it!

name: Francium
description: Francium is an alkali metal and the second rarest naturally occurring element. It is extremely radioactive; its most stable isotope has a half-life of only 22 minutes. At any time, there is less than 30 grams of francium on Earth.
funFact: Francium is so radioactive and short-lived that the largest amount ever assembled in a lab was just 300,000 atoms — less than you can see with any microscope!

name: Radium
description: Radium is a radioactive element discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie. It glows faintly blue in the dark due to its radioactivity causing the air around it to glow. Radium was once used in luminous watch dials, which caused cancer in the Radium Girls.
funFact: Marie Curie's notebooks are still so radioactive that they're kept in lead-lined boxes — you must sign a waiver to look at them!

name: Actinium
description: Actinium is a radioactive element that occurs in nature as an extremely rare trace element in uranium ore. Pure actinium glows blue in the dark due to its intense radioactivity causing air molecules to luminesce. It is the first actinide element.
funFact: Actinium glows a ghostly blue in the dark — not because it's coloured, but because its radioactivity causes surrounding air molecules to emit light!

name: Thorium
description: Thorium is a weakly radioactive, lustrous, silvery-white metal. It was once used in gas mantles in camping lanterns. Thorium is explored as a safer nuclear fuel because it is more abundant than uranium and produces less long-lived waste.
funFact: Thorium could power the entire world's energy needs for thousands of years — it's 3× more abundant than uranium and produces far less radioactive waste!

name: Protactinium
description: Protactinium is a dense, silvery-grey actinide metal which readily reacts with oxygen, water vapour and inorganic acids. It slowly tarnishes in air. It is toxic and radioactive, making it one of the most hazardous elements.
funFact: Protactinium is one of the rarest and most expensive naturally occurring elements — it's also one of the least studied due to its extreme toxicity and radioactivity!

name: Uranium
description: Uranium is a silvery-grey metal that is weakly radioactive. It is the heaviest naturally occurring element. Uranium-235 is the only naturally occurring fissile isotope — it can sustain a nuclear chain reaction and is used as nuclear fuel.
funFact: The energy in just 1 kg of uranium-235 equals the energy from burning 3,000 tonnes of coal — nuclear power is that efficient!

name: Neptunium
description: Neptunium is a radioactive actinide metal. It was the first synthetic transuranium element of the actinide series. Its symbol Np is derived from the planet Neptune, as it follows uranium in the periodic table (just as Neptune follows Uranus).
funFact: Neptunium was synthetically created in a lab in 1940 — it was the first element ever made that doesn't naturally occur on Earth!

name: Plutonium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Arthur Wahl, Joseph W. Kennedy
description: Plutonium is a radioactive actinide metal. It is fissile, like uranium-235, and was used in nuclear weapons. The bomb dropped on Nagasaki used plutonium. It is also used as fuel in nuclear reactors and in spacecraft via radioisotope thermoelectric generators.
funFact: Plutonium-238 powers the Voyager probes — launched in 1977, they're still sending data from interstellar space 45+ years later, all powered by plutonium!

name: Americium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Leon O. Morgan
description: Americium is a radioactive transuranic chemical element. A common household use of americium is in ionisation-type smoke detectors, where a tiny amount of americium-241 is used to detect smoke.
funFact: Your smoke detector almost certainly contains americium-241 — a radioactive element protecting you from fire every day in your home!

name: Curium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Albert Ghiorso
description: Curium is a hard, dense, silvery metal with a relatively high melting point and boiling point for an actinide. It is named after Marie and Pierre Curie. It is intensely radioactive and is produced in nuclear reactors.
funFact: Curium was named in honour of Marie and Pierre Curie — it was actually discovered before Pierre's element curium was named after Marie!

name: Berkelium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Stanley G. Thompson, Albert Ghiorso
description: Berkelium is a transuranic radioactive element and is the actinide element with the atomic number 97. It was first synthesised in December 1949 at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory and named after the city of Berkeley, California.
funFact: Berkelium was used to create element 117 (tennessine) — it took 2 years just to create enough berkelium to start the experiment!

name: Californium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Stanley G. Thompson, Kenneth Street Jr., Albert Ghiorso
description: Californium is a radioactive chemical element. It is a powerful neutron emitter. It was named after California, USA. One microgram of californium-252 emits 170 million neutrons per minute.
funFact: A single gram of californium-252 costs about $27 million — making it one of the most expensive substances on Earth!

name: Einsteinium
description: Einsteinium is a synthetic element produced in nuclear reactors or by bombarding lighter elements with neutrons. It was named after Albert Einstein. It was discovered in the debris of the first hydrogen bomb test.
funFact: Einsteinium was first discovered in the fallout of the first hydrogen bomb explosion in 1952 — scientists found it in the radioactive debris!

name: Fermium
description: Fermium is a synthetic element created by bombarding lighter elements with neutrons. It was named after nuclear physicist Enrico Fermi. Like einsteinium, it was discovered in debris from the first hydrogen bomb test.
funFact: Fermium was also discovered hidden in the debris of a hydrogen bomb blast — two elements discovered from a single nuclear explosion!

name: Mendelevium
discoveredBy: Glenn T. Seaborg, Albert Ghiorso, Gregory Choppin, Bernard Harvey
description: Mendelevium is a synthetic element produced by bombarding einsteinium with helium ions. It was named after Dmitri Mendeleev, the creator of the periodic table. The first 17 atoms of mendelevium ever detected were produced one at a time.
funFact: Mendelevium was named after Mendeleev — the man who invented the periodic table itself! The first batch produced was just 17 atoms.

name: Nobelium
description: Nobelium is a synthetic element. Very little is known about it because only small amounts have been produced. It was named after Alfred Nobel, the inventor of dynamite and founder of the Nobel Prize.
funFact: Nobelium is named after the same Alfred Nobel who created the Nobel Prize — so the Nobel Prize in Chemistry is indirectly connected to this element!

name: Lawrencium
description: Lawrencium is a synthetic chemical element and the final member of the actinide series. It was named after Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron. Its most stable isotope has a half-life of 27.8 hours.
funFact: Lawrencium closes out the actinide series — it's the last element whose chemistry follows the actinide pattern before the super-heavy elements begin!

name: Rutherfordium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
description: Rutherfordium is a synthetic element named after physicist Ernest Rutherford. It is a highly radioactive metal that does not occur in nature. It is classified as a transition metal in group 4.
funFact: Rutherfordium was at the centre of a naming dispute between the US and Soviet Union during the Cold War — each side claimed to have discovered it first!

name: Dubnium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
description: Dubnium is a synthetic chemical element named after the city of Dubna, Russia, home of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research. It is a highly radioactive metal. Its most stable isotope has a half-life of about 28 hours.
funFact: Dubnium is named after Dubna, Russia — there was another Cold War dispute about this element, with the Americans calling it Hahnium instead!

name: Seaborgium
description: Seaborgium is a synthetic chemical element named after chemist Glenn T. Seaborg, who developed the actinide concept. Seaborg was the first living chemist to have an element named after him. It has no known practical applications.
funFact: Glenn Seaborg is the only scientist to have an element named after him while still alive — he reportedly called it the greatest honour of his life!

name: Bohrium
description: Bohrium is a synthetic chemical element named after physicist Niels Bohr. Only a few dozen atoms of bohrium have ever been created. Its most stable isotope has a half-life of about 61 seconds.
funFact: Only a few dozen atoms of bohrium have ever been created in human history — and each atom exists for less than a minute!

name: Hassium
description: Hassium is a synthetic chemical element named after Hassia, the Latin name for the German state of Hesse. It is one of the heaviest elements. Hassium is predicted to be a solid metal at standard conditions.
funFact: Hassium is named after the German state of Hesse (Hassia in Latin) — and scientists predicted it would be so dense that a teaspoon would weigh over 200 grams!

name: Meitnerium
description: Meitnerium is a synthetic element named after physicist Lise Meitner, who co-discovered nuclear fission. It is one of the heaviest synthetic elements. Only a few atoms have ever been created and it decays within milliseconds.
funFact: Lise Meitner co-discovered nuclear fission but was overlooked for the Nobel Prize — element 109, meitnerium, was named in her honour decades later!

name: Darmstadtium
description: Darmstadtium is a synthetic chemical element named after Darmstadt, Germany, where it was discovered. It is radioactive and extremely unstable. Only a few atoms have been produced and they decay within microseconds.
funFact: Darmstadtium was created by bombarding nickel ions into lead atoms at extremely high speeds — a feat of atomic billiards!

name: Roentgenium
description: Roentgenium is a synthetic chemical element named after physicist Wilhelm Röntgen, discoverer of X-rays. It is radioactive and has only been created in a laboratory. Its most stable isotope has a half-life of about 26 seconds.
funFact: Roentgenium is named after the discoverer of X-rays — fittingly, scientists use particle accelerators (a type of radiation technology) to create it!

name: Copernicium
description: Copernicium is named after astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus. It is a radioactive synthetic element. Theoretical calculations suggest it may be a gas at room temperature, unlike all other metals in its group.
funFact: Copernicium might actually be a gas at room temperature — making it the only element in its column of the periodic table that isn't a solid metal!

name: Nihonium
description: Nihonium is a synthetic chemical element first discovered at RIKEN (Japan). Its name comes from 'Nihon', meaning Japan in Japanese — it is the first element discovered in Asia. It decays within milliseconds of production.
funFact: Nihonium is Japan's element — the first element ever discovered in Asia, and it took 9 years and 400 billion bombarded zinc atoms to create just 3 atoms of it!

name: Flerovium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
description: Flerovium is a superheavy synthetic chemical element named after Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions. Theorists believe it may be a gas or behave like a noble gas at room temperature, unlike other elements in its group.
funFact: Flerovium might behave like a noble gas even though it sits in the carbon family — the heavier elements get weirder the further you go in the periodic table!

name: Moscovium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
description: Moscovium is a synthetic element named after Moscow Oblast, Russia. It is radioactive and super-heavy. Only a very small number of atoms have been created in the laboratory.
funFact: Moscovium's name was inspired by Moscow — one of the national prestige elements claimed by Russia as part of the modern periodic table!

name: Livermorium
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
description: Livermorium is a synthetic chemical element named after Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in California, USA. It is a superheavy radioactive element that decays within milliseconds. It is the heaviest known element in group 16.
funFact: Livermorium was created by a US-Russian team — a rare collaboration between former Cold War enemies, jointly naming it after a Californian lab!

name: Tennessine
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Vanderbilt University
description: Tennessine is a synthetic chemical element named after the state of Tennessee, USA. It was officially confirmed in 2010. Tennessine is placed in the halogen group, but it is expected to behave more like a metal than a typical halogen.
funFact: Tennessine is so heavy it belongs to the halogen family — yet scientists predict it will behave more like a metal than chlorine or fluorine!

name: Oganesson
discoveredBy: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory
description: Oganesson is the heaviest element on the periodic table, sitting at the end of period 7. It is named after nuclear physicist Yuri Oganessian. It is predicted to be a solid at room temperature unlike other noble gases, and may have unusual chemical properties.
funFact: Oganesson is the heaviest element ever created — and despite being in the noble gas column, scientists predict it may actually form chemical compounds!
